dialogue: Customer: My laptop keeps freezing when I try to run video editing software. It's a Dell XPS 15, model 2023. Agent: I understand the frustration. Customer: Okay, I'm doing that now. It says it's rolling back to the previous version. Agent: Perfect. Once it's finished, restart your laptop and test Premiere Pro. Let me know if the freezing persists. Customer: It worked! The software is running smoothly now. Thank you so much. Agent: You're welcome. If you have any other issues, don't hesitate to contact us. dialogue:
Customer: Hi, my HP OfficeJet Pro 9015e won't connect to my WiFi. I've been trying for two hours. It's a new router from Xfinity, model XB8, installed on January 10, 2025. My laptop connects fine, but the printer keeps saying 'connection failed.' Can you help?
Agent: Absolutely, I'm Sarah from Tech Support. I understand how frustrating this can be. Let's start by confirming the printer model and your network name. Do you see the 'HP Easy Scan' app on your phone or computer, and is your printer's control panel showing the WiFi setup menu?
Customer: Yes, the printer shows the WiFi setup. I'm using an iPhone 15 Pro with the HP Smart app. The network is 'Xfinity_5G_Home' and password is 'MyPass2025!'. When I enter it, the printer spins for a minute then says 'unable to join network.' I've already rebooted the router twice today.
Agent: Thanks for the details, John. That sounds like a frequency band issue. The XB8 router broadcasts both 2.4GHz and 5GHz. Your printer only supports 2.4GHz, but your phone may be on 5GHz. Let's try this: on your router admin page at 10.0.0.1, disable the 'Band Steering' feature. Also, please check if your printer firmware is up to date—what's the current version shown on the printer's settings?
Customer: I'm looking at the printer info. Firmware version is 2024.12.02. I don't have the router admin password handy—it's on the sticker on the router, but the router is in the basement. Can I do this from the HP Smart app instead?
Agent: The HP Smart app can help, but for the band issue you'll need the router settings. Let me send you a direct link to the Xfinity app that can disable band steering remotely. Meanwhile, try this workaround: on your printer, go to Settings > Network > WiFi Setup, and manually select 'Xfinity_2G_Home'—you can create a separate 2.4GHz SSID from the Xfinity app if it's not visible. Also, please confirm your account email so I can email you a step-by-step guide.
Customer: My email is j.doe123@outlook.com. I just checked the Xfinity app—I see an option to 'split bands' under Advanced Settings. I'll toggle that on. Also, I noticed the printer's IP address is 169.254.12.34, which I think means it's not getting a valid IP from the router?
Agent: Great catch! That 169.254.x.x address confirms it's not getting a DHCP lease. After you split the bands, please power cycle the printer (unplug for 60 seconds), then reconnect to the new 'Xfinity_2G_Home' network. I'll also send you a direct printer firmware update file via email. If this doesn't work by 4 PM today, we can schedule a remote session for tomorrow, January 22, at 10 AM.
Customer: Okay, I'll try that now. One more thing—my printer warranty expired last month. Is there any charge for this support?
Agent: No charge at all, John. Since this is a configuration issue and not hardware failure, support is covered under your HP account's standard assistance. I've noted your case ID: HP-2025-01-21-7829. Please call back if you need more help.
summary:

## Key Facts

dialogue:
Customer: Hi, this is Sarah Mitchell. I'm calling because my email account has been compromised. I noticed it about two hours ago when my friend, Jake, called me asking why I sent him a weird link about cryptocurrency. I checked my sent folder and there are over 200 emails I didn't write, all sent to my entire contact list in the last hour. Customer: I'm really worried—some of those contacts are clients for my freelance graphic design business. Can you help me secure my account?
Agent: Hello Sarah, I'm sorry to hear that. Let me start by verifying your identity. Can you please confirm the email address associated with the account? And for security, could you provide the last four digits of the phone number you used during registration?
Customer: Sure, my email is sarah.mitchell.designs@example.com. The last four digits of my phone number are 7721.
Agent: Thank you, Sarah. I can see the account now. There have been login attempts from an IP address in Russia at 3:15 PM today, and then a mass email blast starting at 3:45 PM. I've locked the account to prevent further damage. First, I need you to reset your password immediately. I'll send a recovery code to your backup email—can you confirm that backup email is jake.roberts@example.com?
Customer: Yes, that's correct. But I already tried resetting my password on my own about 30 minutes ago, and it didn't work. The hacker must have changed the recovery options. Also, I've been getting texts from my bank—someone tried to use my debit card for a $450 purchase at a store in Chicago, but I'm in Portland. Is that related?
Agent: That sounds connected. The hacker may have accessed more than just your email if you used the same password elsewhere. I've just sent the recovery code to jake.roberts@example.com—please check with Jake to get that code. Once you reset the password, enable two-factor authentication. For the bank issue, I recommend calling them immediately at 1-800-555-0199 and freezing your card. Also, I see that the spam emails were sent with a link to a fake Coinbase login page. Have you clicked on any suspicious links recently?
Customer: No, I haven't. But I did receive a strange email yesterday from "support@email-verification.com" asking me to confirm my account. I deleted it without clicking. Could that be how they got in? Also, I have a project due tomorrow for a client named Dr. Patel—he's on my contact list. I need to warn him not to open any links from me.
Agent: Yes, that phishing email is likely the source. The domain "email-verification.com" is known for credential theft. I'm creating a support ticket for you—ticket number TEC-2023-8842. I'll also add a note to forward any further suspicious emails to our security team at abuse@example.com. For now, please change your password as soon as you get the code, enable 2FA, and then scan your computer with antivirus software. I'll escalate this to our fraud department to monitor for any linked accounts. Do you have any other questions?
Customer: Yes, one more thing—how do I send a mass apology email to my contacts? I feel terrible that they got spammed. And do I need to worry about my domain being blacklisted? I use sarahmitchelldesigns.com for my business.
Agent: Good questions. Once your account is secure, you can log in and compose a brief apology email. I recommend sending it in BCC to all contacts to avoid further issues. Regarding your domain, I'll check its reputation now—stand by... It's currently flagged as suspicious, but not blacklisted. After you secure the account and remove all unauthorized forwarding rules, it should recover in 24 to 48 hours. I'll also set up a monitoring alert for your domain. Anything else?
Customer: No, I think that covers it. Thank you so much for your help—I'll call my bank right now and follow your instructions. I really appreciate the quick response.
Agent: You're welcome, Sarah. I'll send a follow-up email with a summary of steps and your ticket number within ten minutes. Take care and stay safe online.

I’ve pulled up your account, and I can see the order ORD-78432 for a wireless keyboard and mouse set. Customer (Alex Morgan): Hi, I’m at gate B12 in Chicago O’Hare, and my connecting flight to Denver at 5:45 PM was supposed to leave 20 minutes ago, but the board says it’s delayed to 8:10 PM. I’m going to miss my connection to San Francisco at 9:15 PM—what can you do?

Agent (Sarah Chen): I’m sorry about that, Alex. Let me pull up your reservation. Can I have your full name and booking reference?

Customer: Alex Morgan, booking reference FLT-8821. I was on the incoming from New York—flight AA 310, landed at 4:20 PM, but we sat on the tarmac for an hour due to storms.

Agent: Thanks, Alex. I see your Denver-to-San Francisco leg is UA 2043 departing at 9:15 PM. Unfortunately, with your new arrival at 8:10 PM in Denver, you’ll miss that. Let me check options—there’s a later UA flight at 11:30 PM arriving SF at 1:15 AM, but it’s showing full. Alternatively, I can rebook you on a morning flight tomorrow, January 15th, at 6:00 AM, arriving 7:45 AM. No change fee, but you’d need a hotel.

Customer: That 6 AM sounds okay, but I have a meeting at 9 AM in SF. Will the airline cover a hotel? Also, I have a bag checked to Denver—where’s that going?

Agent: Yes, since the delay is weather-related, we can provide a hotel voucher for the Denver Airport Marriott, and I’ll tag your bag to be held in Denver for the morning flight. I’ll also issue a meal voucher for $15. Let me confirm the rebooking—UA 2043 at 11:30 PM still has one seat? Hold on… Actually, it just opened up! I can put you on that tonight, arriving 1:15 AM. Would you prefer that?

Customer: Absolutely, put me on the 11:30 PM. I’d rather get in tonight. What about my bag?

Agent: Great, I’ve rebooked you under the same record, and I’ll note your bag to be transferred to UA 2043. Your new boarding pass is available in the app. I also see you’ll have a 3-hour layover in Denver—use the United Club if you have access, or I can offer a $10 lounge pass credit for the inconvenience. Is there anything else?

Customer: That’s perfect, thank you. Just one thing—my original ticket was a roundtrip, will this affect my return from SF on Sunday?

Agent: No, your return on January 18th at 2:15 PM is unchanged. I’ve added a note to your profile about today’s disruption. You should be all set. Safe travels, Alex!

Customer: Thanks, Sarah—really appreciate the quick help.
summary:

Agent (James): Good afternoon, Maria. Thank you for contacting us immediately. I see the pending transfer here on your account ending in 7890. The recipient's email is d.chen@fastmail.com. Let me place a temporary hold on outgoing transactions now. Have you shared your login credentials or received any suspicious calls recently?
Customer (Maria Lopez): No, I haven't given my info to anyone. But yesterday, I got a text saying my account was locked and to click a link to verify. I didn't click it—I called the number on my card instead. Could that be related?
Agent (James): That's very likely a phishing attempt, Maria. Good call not clicking the link. Unfortunately, the transfer already posted to Zelle's network. Since it's over $500, I'm escalating this to our fraud department immediately. I've created case number F-2024-8873. Can you confirm your date of birth and last four of your SSN for verification?

I'm also issuing a provisional credit of $2,500 to your account now—you'll see it within 2 hours. I'm blocking the recipient 'David Chen' from future transfers to your account. Please monitor your account for 30 days and change your online banking password immediately.
Customer (Maria Lopez): Thank you so much, James. I'll change my password right now. Should I also contact Zelle directly?

Agent (Sarah Chen): Good morning, Alex. I'm sorry to hear about the accident. Let me pull up your policy. I see you have comprehensive coverage with a $500 deductible. Can you tell me what happened and where the accident occurred?
Customer: It happened around 4:30 PM at the intersection of Oak and Maple streets in Springfield. I was going straight through a green light when a red sedan, a 2022 Toyota Camry, ran the red light and hit my driver's side door. The other driver is claiming I was at fault, but my dashcam shows the light was green for at least three seconds before I entered the intersection.

dialogue:
Customer (Sarah Mitchell): Hi, I’m calling because my home water damage claim—number CLM-48291—was denied, and I don’t understand why. The adjuster said something about ‘gradual damage,’ but this was from a burst pipe on January 15, 2024. Can you explain?

Agent (James Chen): Good afternoon, Sarah. I’m James Chen, a claims specialist. I see your policy number POL-7723A. The denial letter, dated February 20, 2024, cites section 4.2 of your policy: ‘Exclusions for damage caused by continuous or repeated seepage over 14 days.’ The adjuster noted the pipe had a slow leak for approximately 20 days before the burst. That’s based on the plumber’s report from Apex Plumbing—invoice #AP-3409, dated January 18, which found corrosion.

Customer: That’s not true! I had a plumber, Mark from Reliable Repairs, come out on January 16. He said the pipe burst suddenly due to freezing temperatures—it was 12°F that week. He gave me estimate #RR-221 for $8,500 in repairs. The floor and drywall are ruined. How is that gradual?

Agent: I understand your frustration. Unfortunately, the adjuster’s investigation—report #ADJ-101, filed by adjuster Karen Lee—notes water stains in the basement that predate the burst. She visited on January 19 and photographed mold under the carpet, suggesting moisture for at least 2-3 weeks. The policy excludes gradual damage, even if a sudden event triggered it. Also, your policy has a 30-day notification clause; you reported it on January 17, which is fine, but the evidence contradicts sudden onset.

Customer: That’s ridiculous. I’ve lived here for 10 years and never had mold. Can I appeal? What do I need to send?

Agent: Yes, you can appeal. You have until March 21, 2024—30 days from the denial. Send a written letter with supporting documents: plumber’s report from Reliable Repairs, photos from before the leak (if any), and a signed statement from Mark. Also, include a copy of the denial letter. Our appeals department at P.O. Box 4400, Hartford, CT 06101 handles that. You can also call 1-800-555-0199 for status updates.

Customer: Okay, I’ll do that. But what about my temporary housing? I’m staying at a motel—it’s costing $150 a night. Your policy says loss of use is covered for sudden damage.

Agent: Loss of use is covered only if the damage is from a covered peril. Since we denied the claim, that benefit is also denied. However, if the appeal is successful, we can reimburse up to $5,000 for temporary housing—that’s your policy limit. I’d recommend keeping all receipts, dated from January 17 onward.

Customer: Alright, I’ll gather everything. One last thing—the claim was denied by Karen Lee. Can I request a different adjuster for the appeal?

Agent: I can note that, but appeals are typically reviewed by a senior adjuster, not the original one. I’ll add a note: ‘Customer requests new adjuster for impartiality.’ Your appeal case number will be APP-5592. Anything else?

Customer: No, thanks. I’ll send the documents by next Friday. Goodbye.

Agent: Goodbye, Sarah. I hope the appeal works out.

Customer (Sarah Mitchell): Hi, this is Sarah Mitchell. I stayed at your hotel from March 10th to March 12th in room 412, and I just saw a charge of $48.50 on my credit card for minibar items. I didn't consume anything from the minibar during my stay. Can you help me understand this?
Agent (James): Good afternoon, Ms. Mitchell. I'm sorry to hear about the confusion. Let me pull up your account. Could you confirm the last four digits of the card you used?
Customer: Sure, it's 7892.
Agent: Thank you. I can see the charge was posted on March 13th for two bottles of water ($12 each), a bag of chips ($6.50), and a small bottle of wine ($18). That totals $48.50. The record shows these were scanned from your room's minibar sensors on March 12th at 9:15 PM.
Customer: That's impossible. I was out to dinner with my husband at Bella Napoli from 7:00 PM until 10:30 PM that night. We came back, went straight to bed, and checked out at 7 AM the next day. There's no way those items were taken by us.

Customer: Yes, I'll hold.
Agent (after a moment): Ms. Mitchell, I found the issue. Our housekeeping supervisor noted that on March 12th, a staff member restocked the minibar and inadvertently reset the sensor without clearing the previous scan from the guest in room 411, who checked out that morning and had consumed those items. The charge was applied to your room in error. I sincerely apologize for the mistake.
Customer: Oh, I see. That's a relief to know it wasn't something we did. So what happens now?

Customer: Thank you, James. I appreciate you looking into this and resolving it so quickly. I'll keep an eye on my statement.
Agent: You're welcome, Ms. Mitchell. Again, my apologies for the error. Have a wonderful day.

Customer: Hi, this is Sarah Jenkins. I rented an electric vehicle from your Downtown Portland location yesterday, March 15th, reservation number #EV-78432. The car was supposed to be fully charged, but when I picked it up, the battery was at only 18%. I assumed it would be fine for my short trip, but now I'm stuck on Highway 26 near the town of Sandy with 2% battery left, and the nearest charging station is 14 miles away according to my map. What are you going to do about this?

Agent: Good afternoon, Sarah. This is Michael at GreenRide EV Rentals. I'm very sorry to hear about your situation. Let me pull up your reservation. #EV-78432, correct? I see you rented a 2024 Nissan Leaf for a 3-day period ending March 18th. You're right—the pre-rental inspection notes show the battery was at 18%, which is unacceptable. Our policy is to provide vehicles with at least 80% charge. I apologize for the oversight. Let me check our roadside assistance options. Do you have a membership with any charging networks like ChargePoint or Electrify America?

Customer: No, I don't have any memberships—I'm from out of town, visiting for a conference. I paid $287.50 upfront for the rental, plus a $150 deposit. I specifically requested a fully charged EV because I have a meeting in Bend tomorrow morning at 9 AM. Right now, my car is in a rest area, and the temperature is dropping. I can't even run the heater without draining the battery further. Is there a tow truck or a mobile charging service you can send?

Agent: I completely understand your frustration, Sarah. I've already contacted our roadside partner, RoadGuard Towing. They have a mobile charging unit available, but their estimated arrival time is 45 minutes due to your location. I'm also waiving the standard $75 roadside assistance fee. Additionally, I'm issuing a $100 credit to your account for the inconvenience and the charging failure. In the meantime, can you confirm your exact GPS coordinates so I can relay them to the driver? And just so you know, the nearest working Level 3 charger is at the Shell station in Sandy, but it's 14 miles east. Our mobile unit will bring enough charge to get you there safely.

Customer: The GPS coordinates are 45.3976° N, 122.2615° W. I'm parked near the 'Sandy River Rest Area' sign. Thank you for the credit, but I'm more concerned about making it to Bend. What if the mobile charger doesn't arrive in time, or the battery is too degraded to hold a charge? Can you upgrade me to a different vehicle? I can't miss my meeting—it's with a major client, and it cost me $800 in travel expenses to get here.

Agent: Those coordinates are perfect, Sarah. I've sent them to the tow driver, who is en route and should be with you by 4:10 PM Pacific time. To address your concerns, I'm upgrading your reservation to a 2024 Hyundai Ioniq 6 with a 310-mile range, which we'll have ready for you at our Portland location by 6 PM tonight. You can swap the Nissan Leaf for the Ioniq when you return to Portland after your meeting. There's no additional charge for the upgrade, and I'm extending your rental period by one day at no cost, so your new return date is March 19th. Does that work for you?

Customer: That sounds reasonable, but I'm worried about the logistics. I have to drive back to Portland from Sandy, which is about 30 miles, then switch cars, and then drive 160 miles to Bend. With the mobile charge, will the Leaf make it to Portland? And what about the charging at the dealership? I don't want to waste another hour there.

Agent: The mobile unit will provide a 40-mile range charge, which is more than enough to get you to our Portland lot. I'll also pre-authorize a priority check-in at our service bay so you can swap cars in under 10 minutes. The Ioniq will be fully charged and waiting. Additionally, I'm adding a free 30-day membership to Electrify America to your account, which covers the charger in Sandy if you need a top-off before heading to Bend. Your total compensation for this incident will be the $100 credit plus a $50 fuel voucher for the inconvenience. I'll send all confirmations to the email on file. Is there anything else I can help with?

Customer: No, that covers everything. I appreciate you taking this seriously and the quick solution. I'll wait for the tow truck. Thank you, Michael.

Agent: You're welcome, Sarah. I'll monitor the tow progress and send you a text when the driver is 5 minutes out. Safe travels to Bend, and please don't hesitate to call back if you need any further assistance. Goodbye.

Customer (Alex): Hi, I'm calling about a rental I returned yesterday at your Downtown branch. The final bill shows a $65 fuel charge, but I filled the tank just before returning it. I have the receipt showing $42.50 at a Shell station at 10th and Main, about three miles from your lot. My rental agreement number is R-78421. 

Agent (Jordan): Good morning, Alex. I appreciate you providing those details. Let me pull up your agreement. I see the fuel charge was added because upon inspecting the vehicle at return—a blue 2023 Honda Civic, license plate JKL-789—the fuel gauge read just under half full, not full. Our policy is that if the tank isn't full, we charge a pre-set rate of $7.50 per gallon plus a $15 service fee. The tank holds about 12 gallons, so we assessed roughly 6 gallons missing. That accounts for the $65. 

Customer: That doesn't make sense. I swear I filled it. The pump at Shell showed 10.2 gallons added, bringing it from near empty to full. I even took a photo of the pump reading and the odometer—it's 32,451 miles. The tank should have been completely full when I pulled into your lot. Could there be a mistake with the gauge? Maybe it wasn't calibrated or someone else drove it after I parked? 

Agent: I understand your frustration. Let me check the return photos. Our lot attendant takes timestamped photos at drop-off. I'm looking at the image now—the gauge needle is pointing just below the 'F' mark, closer to the halfway mark between 'E' and 'F'. I can see the odometer reads 32,452, so that matches your mileage. The tank might have been filled, but the gauge could be faulty, or perhaps there's a slight incline in our lot that affects the reading. We had a similar complaint last week. 

Customer: Okay, so if the gauge is off, why am I being penalized? I have proof I paid for a full tank. Can you check if the previous renter reported any issues with the fuel gauge? Or can you reimburse the $65 and we can test the gauge together? I'm willing to drive back to the lot to demonstrate. 

Agent: That's a fair point. Unfortunately, I can't override the system without manager approval. Let me transfer you to my supervisor, Maria. She can investigate the gauge issue and possibly authorize a refund. One moment, please. 

Maria (Supervisor): Hello Alex, I've reviewed the notes and photos. I also checked the vehicle's service history—there's no record of a gauge problem, but I agree the angle in the photo suggests the car might have been parked on a slight slope. To resolve this, I'll waive the $65 fuel charge today as a goodwill adjustment, given your receipt. I'll also flag the vehicle for a gauge calibration check. You should see the credit on your card within 3-5 business days. Is there anything else I can help with? 

Customer: That's very helpful, thank you. Just to confirm, the $65 will be refunded, and I won't be charged any additional fees for this? 

Maria: Correct. The charge will be removed, and you won't owe anything extra. I'll send you a confirmation email within the hour. We apologize for the inconvenience. 

Customer: Great, I appreciate the quick resolution. I'll look out for the email. Thanks again.

dialogue:
User: Hey, I’m having a nightmare with my VPN connection while working remotely. It keeps dropping every 10 to 15 minutes, and I lose access to the company’s internal servers. I’m using the corporate Cisco AnyConnect client on a Windows 10 laptop, and my home internet is fiber optic, supposedly stable at 200 Mbps. I’ve already rebooted my router and modem twice today, but it still cuts out. Can you help?

Support: I hear you—that’s frustrating. Let’s start with some specifics. When the VPN drops, do you get any error message in the AnyConnect client, like ‘Connection lost’ or ‘SSL handshake failed’? Also, does your local internet connection stay active during those drops? Let’s verify by opening a command prompt and pinging 8.8.8.8 continuously while the VPN is down.

User: Good question. Yes, the local internet stays up—I can still browse websites and stream video on my phone. The AnyConnect client shows ‘Disconnected’ with a generic message: ‘Secure Gateway disconnected.’ No SSL errors. I did the ping test: when the VPN drops, pings to 8.8.8.8 are fine, but pings to our company server 10.20.30.40 go to ‘Request timed out.’ It’s like the tunnel just collapses.

Support: That narrows it down. Likely a timeout or MTU issue. Let’s check your MTU settings. Open AnyConnect, go to Preferences > Statistics, and look at the MTU size under the connection details. Also, try adjusting the VPN profile to force a smaller MTU—like 1400 bytes instead of 1500. I’ll walk you through: click the gear icon in AnyConnect, go to ‘Advanced Options,’ then ‘MTU Size,’ and set it to 1400. Reconnect and test for 20 minutes.

User: Okay, I did that. The MTU was set to ‘Auto’ before. I changed it to 1400, reconnected, and it stayed stable for exactly 18 minutes, then dropped again. Same behavior—no error, just disconnect. Pings to the internal server fail right after. I also noticed the AnyConnect log shows ‘SSL/TLS renegotiation failed’ just before the drop. Should I worry about my router?

Support: The ‘SSL/TLS renegotiation failed’ is key. That suggests the VPN gateway is dropping the tunnel due to a certificate or cipher mismatch, or maybe a firewall rule on your home router is interfering. Let’s test with a different network: can you tether your laptop to your phone’s cellular hotspot and connect to the VPN? If it stays up, the issue is your home router. Also, disable IPv6 on your laptop’s network adapter—right-click the network icon, go to ‘Network Settings > Change adapter options,’ right-click your Wi-Fi or Ethernet, Properties, uncheck ‘Internet Protocol Version 6 (TCP/IPv6),’ and retry.

User: Disabling IPv6 didn’t help—still dropped after 12 minutes. I switched to my phone’s hotspot (5G), and the VPN stayed connected for over 30 minutes without issues! So it’s definitely my home router. It’s a TP-Link Archer AX73. I’ve updated the firmware to the latest version, but I haven’t touched any firewall or QoS settings. What should I check?

Support: Great isolation. On the TP-Link Archer AX73, log into the admin interface (usually 192.168.0.1). Go to ‘Advanced > Security > Firewall’ and make sure ‘SPI Firewall’ is enabled—it can cause issues if set too aggressively. Also, disable ‘DoS Protection’ if it’s on. Then, under ‘Advanced > Network > QoS,’ ensure it’s disabled or set to low priority for VPN traffic. Another common fix: enable ‘VPN Passthrough’ for PPTP, L2TP, and IPSec, even though you’re using SSL. Finally, try changing the router’s MTU under ‘Network > WAN’ to 1400 as well. Reboot the router and test for an hour.

User: I did all that. SPI Firewall was already on, DoS Protection was off, QoS was off, VPN Passthrough was enabled by default. I set the WAN MTU to 1400, rebooted, and connected. This time it stayed up for 45 minutes! But then it dropped again, same SSL renegotiation error. I’m losing my mind. Could it be a DNS issue or something with my ISP throttling VPN traffic?

Support: Good progress—45 minutes is better. The SSL renegotiation error still points to a timeout, possibly due to keep-alive settings. In AnyConnect, go to Preferences > General (under the profile) and set ‘Keep Alive’ to 10 seconds and ‘Reconnect After Resume’ to ‘Disconnect.’ Also, try adding a simple DNS entry for your company’s VPN server in your hosts file—though unlikely. For the ISP throttling theory: use a tool like ‘Wireshark’ or run a traceroute to your VPN server (tracert 10.20.30.40) while connected, and see if there are packet drops after a specific hop. But my best bet now is to call your ISP and ask them to disable any ‘Deep Packet Inspection’ or ‘Smart Packet Management’ on your line. Also, consider using a different VPN protocol—if your IT supports it, switch from SSL to IPSec IKEv2 in the AnyConnect profile. Let’s try IKEv2: in AnyConnect, go to Network Access Manager, select the profile, and change the ‘Primary Protocol’ to IKEv2, then reconnect.

User: I switched to IKEv2 as you suggested, and so far it’s been running for 1 hour and 20 minutes without a single drop! The connection feels a bit slower—maybe 10% less throughput—but I’ll take stability over speed. I also called my ISP, and they confirmed they have a feature called ‘Advanced Security’ that sometimes interferes with VPNs; they disabled it on my account. I’ll keep testing for the rest of the day. Thank you—this saved my workday!

Support: Excellent news! The IKEv2 protocol often bypasses SSL-related timeouts. Keep the MTU settings and router config as they are. If it drops again, try lowering the router’s MTU to 1360 or check if the ISP’s ‘Advanced Security’ re-enables after a reboot (some ISPs do that). Otherwise, you’re good. I’ll log a ticket with your company’s IT to note the protocol change for your profile. Happy remote working!
summary:
## Summary
User reported frequent VPN disconnects (every 10-15 min) using Cisco AnyConnect on Windows 10 over a TP-Link Archer AX73 router with fiber internet. Initial troubleshooting ruled out local internet issues and MTU settings (set to 1400). Disabling IPv6 and testing via phone hotspot identified the home router as the culprit. Router firewall/QoS adjustments (SPI enable, DoS disable, VPN Passthrough enable, WAN MTU 1400) improved uptime to 45 min but still dropped with SSL/TLS renegotiation errors. Switching from SSL to IKEv2 protocol and contacting ISP to disable 'Advanced Security' (which interfered with VPN traffic) resolved the issue, with stable connection for over 1 hour 20 min.

## Key Facts
- VPN client: Cisco AnyConnect; error: 'SSL/TLS renegotiation failed'.
- Local internet stable; pings to internal server (10.20.30.40) fail on drop.
- Router: TP-Link Archer AX73; firmware up-to-date; SPI firewall and VPN Passthrough were already correctly set.
- Phone hotspot test confirmed router-specific issue.
- ISP feature 'Advanced Security' was interfering; disabled after request.
- Protocol change to IKEv2 yielded stable connection (1h20min+).

## Decisions
- Set AnyConnect MTU to 1400 (from Auto).
- Disabled IPv6 on laptop network adapter.
